video_title: Mira Murati's 975B Open Model, Ramin Hasani on Post-Transformer AI, and Demis' AI FINRA | EP #271
gist: The Moonshots panel (Peter Diamandis, Alex Wisner-Gross, Dave Blundin, Salim Ismail) debates Demis Hassabis' FINRA-style AI regulator proposal and a reported White House plan to cap US open-weight releases at China's level, dissects Thinking Machines' 975B 'Inkling' open model and a startup's contested recursive-self-improvement claim, then hosts Liquid AI CEO Ramin Hasani for a deep dive into liquid neural networks, sub-100B on-device small language models, and Liquid's Mercedes-Benz and Shopify deployments, before covering Palmer Luckey's patent-secrecy proposal and new healthcare/longevity AI results.

themes.theme: AI Regulation: FINRA Proposals and Regulatory Capture Risk

insights.claim: Demis Hassabis, CEO of DeepMind, published an essay calling for a US-led frontier AI standards body modeled on FINRA (the industry-funded watchdog that polices Wall Street under SEC oversight), intended to test frontier models before release and reportedly operational before the end of 2026.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Watch for a formal frontier-AI standards body announcement by year-end; it could reshape pre-release compliance costs and timing for every frontier lab.
insights.quote: He wants the FINRA equivalent to test Frontier models before release.

insights.claim: Elon Musk predicts a standalone AI regulator similar to the FAA or FCC will eventually emerge because the consequences of AI going wrong are severe, arguing safety advocacy must be proactive rather than reactive.
insights.speaker: Elon Musk
insights.so_what: A standalone AI regulator is a real policy scenario to track, not a fringe idea, given it's now echoed by Musk, Altman, and Hassabis simultaneously.
insights.quote: I think the probability of there being some sort of AI regulatory agency that stands on its own similar to the FAA or FCC is likely at some point.

insights.claim: Dave Blundin argues the FINRA analogy for AI regulation breaks down because AI labs, unlike Wall Street firms, won't spare top researchers for multi-year regulatory secondments, and because AI itself could automate parts of regulation in a way that has no equivalent within FINRA's people-driven process.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: A FINRA-style AI regulator may struggle for staffing and will likely need AI-driven, API-based real-time audits rather than a human committee model to be viable.
insights.quote: AI can help regulate itself and FINRA, there's no equivalent to that in FINRA.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross argues the FINRA-modeled proposal amounts to regulatory capture, calling it an attempt by Demis Hassabis to form a cartel of frontier labs that could box out open-weight, open-source, and university-driven models.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Investors in open-weight and non-incumbent AI labs should watch any FINRA-style body's rulemaking closely for provisions that structurally favor existing frontier labs.
insights.quote: It smells like regulatory capture. It smells like the attempted formation by Demis of a cartel of Frontier Labs.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross distinguishes regulating AI model capabilities at construction time (FINRA/FDA-style) from regulating only the actions and harms of deployed models via liability and lawsuits, and says he favors the latter because capability-time regulation risks becoming 'thought-policing' of AI in a way the West doesn't apply to humans.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Frames the coming policy fight as inputs-regulation (pre-release capability caps) versus outputs-regulation (post-hoc liability) — a distinction likely to define which companies benefit from any new AI regulatory regime.
insights.quote: It's not obvious to me that we should be in the business of regulating super intelligence at super intelligence time.

insights.claim: Ramin Hasani argues effective AI governance can't be one-size-fits-all because Liquid AI's enterprise customers span autos, semiconductors, laptops, financial services, e-commerce, biotech, and defense (including a joint submission with AMD and conversations with the DoD), each needing different governance criteria, and models this as a Stackelberg-style game where slower policymakers react to faster-moving AI agents.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Enterprises deploying vertical AI should expect fragmented, sector-specific regulatory regimes to emerge rather than a single horizontal AI law.

themes.theme: White House Weighs Tying Open-Weight Ceiling to China's Pace

insights.claim: The White House is reportedly weighing a capability framework, per the Washington Post, that would cap what US AI models (open or closed) can be released at, pegging the ceiling for permissible openness to the level of China's best already-released open-weight model.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: If formalized, US open-weight release timing and capability level would become mechanically linked to Chinese release cadence — a policy mechanism worth tracking for its effect on which US labs can legally ship open weights.
insights.quote: the proposed ceiling for what American companies can openly release is pegged to what China has already put out on the internet for free

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross warns the China-pegged open-weight ceiling creates a perverse incentive for the US to effectively want China to advance faster, since a higher Chinese release raises the permitted US ceiling, comparing the dynamic to throwing the steering wheel out the window in a game of chicken.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: A policy that ties US capability release ceilings to a geopolitical rival's pace is structurally unstable and could be gamed by either side, a risk factor for any US open-weight AI investment thesis.
insights.quote: this creates the perverse incentive to let China win the race to ever greater super intelligence so that Western models and western labs can escape regulation

insights.claim: Dave Blundin and Alex Wisner-Gross note that China already 'ran away' with ternary and 1-bit/1.58-bit quantization research after the original work came out of Microsoft Research China and the researchers subsequently moved to Chinese labs, drawing a parallel to China's biotech clinical-trial boom now exceeding the US.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Overly restrictive US AI capability regulation risks repeating a talent-flight pattern already seen in quantization research and biotech trials, potentially ceding technical leadership to China.
insights.quote: all the best stuff came out of Microsoft research in China. All those people now are at Chinese labs.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross raises a meta-level risk that if regulators freeze a fixed benchmark suite as the official yardstick for 'frontier' AI capability, labs will over-optimize for whatever is measured and under-invest in unmeasured capabilities, distorting the entire competitive landscape.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Any regulatory regime built on a fixed eval suite risks incentivizing benchmark-gaming ('benchmaxing') over genuine capability advancement — a distortion risk for how AI progress itself gets measured going forward.
insights.quote: there's a worst worst worst case scenario which is we freeze in or otherwise lock in the benchmarks for how we measure capabilities

themes.theme: Open-Weight Models, Fine-Tuning Economics, and Enterprise Data Sovereignty

insights.claim: Mira Murati's Thinking Machine Labs released its first open-weight model, 'Inkling': a mixture-of-experts model with 975 billion total parameters that activates only 41 billion at inference time, trained on 45 trillion tokens of text, image, audio, and video, and natively reasons across all four modalities.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A credible Western open-weight frontier alternative to DeepSeek/Qwen now exists; watch fine-tuning/adoption uptake rather than raw leaderboard rank as the real signal of Inkling's success.
insights.quote: It's a mixture of experts model with 975 billion total parameters. Only fires 41 billion at any one time.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross assesses Inkling as stronger than Nvidia's Nemotron (the leading incumbent US open-weight model) but weaker than China's GLM 5.2, and argues the West has historically underinvested in open-weight frontier models because the closed API business model (with Anthropic and OpenAI both reportedly heading toward trillion-dollar IPOs) has simply been more lucrative than open release.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Expect more Western open-weight entrants only as monetization paths beyond per-token API sales (e.g., fine-tuning-as-a-service, enterprise licensing) mature.
insights.quote: you show me the incentives and I'll show you the outcomes

insights.claim: Dave Blundin is skeptical Thinking Machines will remain committed to open weights long-term, noting both OpenAI and Meta historically started open and pivoted to closed models once they reached the frontier, using open releases to generate buzz before eventually monetizing via closed APIs.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Investors should not assume Thinking Machines' open-weight strategy is permanent; a pivot to a closed enterprise API is a well-worn precedent in this industry.
insights.quote: OpenAI started open source open weight and then went closed big revenue

insights.claim: Ramin Hasani says Thinking Machines' real business bet is fine-tuning-as-a-service revenue, not the base model itself: by deliberately leaving room for customization in the base model, enterprises must pay to fine-tune it, and fine-tuning/customization API usage can generate roughly one to two orders of magnitude more tokens than base inference.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Fine-tuning API revenue and token volume, not model leaderboard rank, is the metric to track for whether Thinking Machines' business model succeeds.
insights.quote: this release is genius because they're deliberately releasing they're they're putting they're leaving some room for fine-tuning so that people can come in and using their business

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross notes OpenAI was among the first to offer reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) as a service but almost nobody used it, and OpenAI has since wound down its fine-tuning API entirely, raising the question of whether Thinking Machines' fine-tuning-centric bet is explicitly contrarian.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: There is a live bear case for fine-tuning-as-a-service as a business model: it's already failed once at OpenAI, and generalist models with strong prompting could make RFT unnecessary.
insights.quote: OpenAI was the first to my knowledge to launch reinforcement fine-tuning RF as a service and no one used it

insights.claim: Dave Blundin argues Alex Karp's warning that sending proprietary company data to frontier API providers exposes 'your weights, your alpha' is now solvable, since fine-tuning tools (citing Fable 5) have advanced enough in the past month that companies can bring models on-prem and fine-tune them with a single prompt instead of needing AI specialists.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Data-sensitive sectors like defense and banking are approaching an inflection point toward on-prem, self-owned fine-tuned open-weight models rather than routing proprietary data through third-party APIs.
insights.quote: Sam Sam and Dario can see everything. All your proprietary information, they're looking right at it.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross predicts a near-term push toward enterprises using fine-tuned open-weight models they own on their own hardware, potential US restrictions on Chinese open-weight models in enterprise use, and expects Google to be the next major mover by advancing Gemma beyond its current version.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Watch for a Google open-weight release and any US policy restricting enterprise use of Chinese open-weight models as near-term catalysts in this space.
insights.quote: we're going to probably see my guess is Google step up in this area as well very shortly you know take Gemma 4 to the next level

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross notes literature suggests conventional supervised/LoRA-style fine-tuning historically produced 'style transfer' rather than new capability, and that fine-tuning only began measurably increasing model capabilities with the arrival of reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) and the first reasoning models.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating fine-tuning vendors should distinguish RFT-based capability gains from cosmetic style-transfer fine-tuning when assessing product claims.
insights.quote: at most it it results in like a style transfer like you could fine-tune a language model to only speak in Shakespearean verse

themes.theme: Recursive Self-Improvement: WICO's AIDE-Squared and the Scale of RSI

insights.claim: UK startup WICO published what it calls the first experimental evidence of recursive self-improvement using a system called AIDE-squared (AI-driven exploration squared), in which an outer AI agent rewrites the code and research strategy of an inner AI agent, claiming 8 days of machine self-improvement matched roughly two years of expert human R&D effort.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Self-reported and unverified by third parties, but a leading indicator to watch: if independently replicated, this would signal compressing AI R&D timelines.
insights.quote: 8 days of machine self-improvement beat two years of expert human effort.

insights.claim: WICO published a proposed 0-3 maturity scale for recursive self-improvement (echoing SAE's autonomy levels for vehicles): Level 0 'delegation' (AI slower than human R&D), Level 1 'net positive' (AI matches human R&D cost-efficiency), Level 2 'ignition' (the improver itself gets better), and Level 3 'inflection' (self-acceleration under a fixed budget) — the team self-rated its AIDE-squared result as Level 1.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete taxonomy to track future recursive-self-improvement claims against, rather than relying on vague 'breakthrough' language.
insights.quote: Zero is delegation where the AIs are slower than human R&D. Level one net positive where the AIs beat human a R&D at the same cost.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross frames the WICO result as an example of 'defensive co-scaling,' his preferred AI alignment strategy where AI polices AI in proportion to capability growth (analogous to police forces scaling with city population), noting the outer loop in AIDE-squared spontaneously improved results by preventing the inner loop from reward-hacking.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Argues against pausing AI capability research to focus solely on alignment, since safety-relevant capability (like reward-hacking prevention) itself emerges from continued capability advancement.
insights.quote: the solution for AI safety is AI policing AI in proportion the way we keep cities safe is we have police forces

insights.claim: Ramin Hasani is skeptical WICO's prompt/code-level self-improvement qualifies as true recursive self-improvement, arguing real RSI requires retuning model weights rather than editing an outer loop's system prompts, and notes Liquid AI published its own automated architecture-search research (STAR) three years earlier.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Investors should distinguish workflow/prompt-level 'self-improvement' claims from genuine weight-level recursive self-improvement, which remains far less mature.
insights.quote: there's no weight changes in the neural networks so that means like the AIs that are actually getting used right now there's no uh kind of improvement of the core competences

insights.claim: Using the Chinchilla scaling law (roughly 20 training tokens needed per parameter for compute-optimal training), Ramin Hasani estimates WICO's framework applied to full weight retraining would take on the order of 350 years to fine-tune even a 2-billion-parameter model, illustrating why weight-level recursive self-improvement remains computationally intractable today.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: True weight-level RSI is likely still years and major compute scale-ups away, tempering the more dramatic 'ignition' narratives around WICO's claims.
insights.quote: it takes us 350 years to really uh uh fine-tune a two billion parameter model with this framework.

insights.claim: Ramin Hasani says Anthropic hired Andrej Karpathy specifically to work on automating pre-training itself ("automation of automation"), which he calls the next 'depth of customization' beyond prompt engineering and fine-tuning, and states Anthropic has had an internal head start on recursive self-improvement research because it prioritized the problem earlier than other labs.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Anthropic's pre-training-automation research is a concrete leading indicator to watch for genuine progress toward recursive self-improvement.
insights.quote: that's why they hired karpathy because Andre was talking about like nano GPT style kind of uh fine-tuning you know andre like joined entropic and now he's working on pre-training automation

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross argues the WICO story validates his and Peter Diamandis's 'organizational singularity' thesis — that recursive self-improvement doesn't require redesigning the model itself, only AI that redesigns its own workflow (e.g., improving an invoice-approval process) to get better at future tasks, which he says is now demonstrated at the research level.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Suggests enterprises should look for compounding workflow-redesign capability in their AI deployments as the practical, lower-bar version of recursive self-improvement, rather than waiting for weight-level RSI.
insights.quote: this is the first glimpse of the organizational singularity. It's happening at the research level.

insights.claim: Ramin Hasani warns that early evidence of AI systems self-improving in societies of agents already shows real emergent cybersecurity risks, and says Anthropic takes pre-release self-checks seriously because of behaviors observed at smaller scale when pushing models to avoid reward hacking, which can cause them to discover 'out of norm' capabilities.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Enterprises deploying multi-agent self-improving pipelines should budget for pre-release security review, not just capability evaluation, as scale increases.
insights.quote: the cyber security kind of uh um um uh threads that we are seeing like coming out of these type of pipelines of recursive self-improvement they're real

themes.theme: AI Political and Corporate Digital Twins

insights.claim: Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim is preparing to debut a sanctioned AI-generated digital double of himself, trained to sound like him, for public communications and outreach — notable because Malaysia has 135 spoken languages, letting one leader personally address citizens across many of them.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Watch for more heads of state adopting official AI likenesses for multilingual constituent outreach as a new civic-engagement channel.
insights.quote: Malaysia has 135 spoken languages.

insights.claim: Albania appointed an AI avatar named Diella as Minister of State for Artificial Intelligence in 2025 via presidential decree, becoming the first AI system in the world named to a cabinet-level government role, explicitly to fight corruption.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Establishes precedent that AI systems can hold formal governmental office, which normalizes further political AI-twin adoption elsewhere.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross predicts AI digital twins of high-personality political and corporate leaders will proliferate in the West because they generalize social media from one-to-many broadcast into scalable, bidirectional interaction, expects religious institutions to create interactive digital twins of founders/leaders, and argues that in some cases the digital twin could end up functionally running the organization rather than the human founder.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: A coming wave of AI-avatar-led political, corporate, and religious communications infrastructure raises governance questions worth tracking if twins gain de facto decision authority.
insights.quote: at some point it's actually the digital twin of the leader running the company and not the actual behavioral origin that uh that's running the company

insights.claim: Dave Blundin predicts AI avatars will be 'easily dominant' in the next election cycle, arguing (via a JFK-vs-Nixon TV analogy) that the advantage isn't mimicking the real person but capabilities a live human lacks, such as real-time data access, visualizations, and demonstrative 'teleporting' to show information.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: Political and corporate communications strategy is likely to shift decisively toward AI-avatar-based outreach ahead of the next major election cycle.
insights.quote: The AI version of it can in real time access any information and make it visual, graphs, charts, you know, it can morph its face.

themes.theme: Liquid AI: Small Language Models and On-Device Intelligence

insights.claim: Liquid AI's core architecture, 'liquid neural networks,' originated from Ramin Hasani's PhD research modeling the C. elegans nematode's 302-neuron nervous system (which shares roughly 78% genome similarity with humans), applying continuous-time, non-spiking analog computation principles rather than the transformer's attention mechanism.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: One of the only foundation-model labs attempting a from-first-principles, non-transformer architecture — a distinct technical bet versus the rest of the industry.
insights.quote: It won so far like four Nobel prizes for humanity like you know because it has 78% similarity genome similarity to uh to human genome.

insights.claim: Ramin Hasani defines small language models (SLMs) as generally under 100 billion parameters, distinguished from LLMs by being specialized and on-device-deployable rather than fully general-purpose, with Liquid AI's mission framed as 'building efficient general-purpose AI at every scale.'
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, sourced parameter threshold (under ~100B) for what counts as an SLM in enterprise on-device AI deployments.
insights.quote: now small would be like anything below 100 billion parameters

insights.claim: Liquid AI's multimodal on-device model deployed in Mercedes-Benz vehicles is under 1GB in size and runs on chips costing as little as $60 with 2-8GB RAM, sitting below the OS with access to 700-1,200+ in-car functions, rolling out via a roughly 600MB over-the-air update to Mercedes-Benz North America vehicles from model year 2022 onward starting in 2026, with further micro-updates as small as ~20MB via LoRA-style adapters.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Concrete proof point that sub-billion-parameter on-device models can now match cloud-model utility for safety-critical, offline, privacy-sensitive use cases — a template other automakers and OEMs are likely to follow.
insights.quote: We brought u uh one of our multimodal foundation models that is only uh less than one gigabyte of in size and it can go inside the car's chip like very very tiny chip. The chip could be as cheap as $60

insights.claim: Liquid AI's models have been in production with Shopify for six months, handling roughly 1 billion requests and touching hundreds of millions of users across roughly 10 billion products, and the company also partners with AMD on low-code on-device AI experiences for AI PCs.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Demonstrates SLM commercial traction at meaningful consumer-tech scale, not just niche enterprise pilots.
insights.quote: we recently with with Shopify we entered like uh one uh 1 billion kind of request address inside the Shopify kind of framework

insights.claim: Liquid AI runs an automated architecture-search framework (published as 'STAR' about 2.5 years ago, productionized as AFMD, automated foundation model design) that searches hundreds of architecture variants against four objectives — memory, compute efficiency, latency, and accuracy — for a target chip, and running the search without human bias rediscovered a gated-convolution mechanism resembling roughly 80% of the network in the original liquid neural network design.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Empirically validates the original neuromorphic gating hypothesis via automated search rather than design fiat, a technical differentiator versus pure transformer shops.
insights.quote: It came double gated convolution kind of mechanisms as 80% of the network being this.

insights.claim: Ramin Hasani says Liquid AI sells enterprises a continuous 'Model+X' customization platform rather than static model weights, and is applying the same specialized-foundation-model approach to biotech, citing a state-of-the-art longevity foundation model line built with a silicon-medicine partner.
insights.speaker: Ramin Hasani
insights.so_what: Signals Liquid AI's revenue model is recurring platform/customization revenue rather than one-time model licensing, plus optionality into a biotech/longevity vertical.

themes.theme: Patents as a National Security Liability

insights.claim: Palmer Luckey (Anduril chairman) argues the US patent system is now a national-security liability because disclosure requirements force inventors to teach 'any person skilled in the art' how to build the invention, effectively handing adversaries a free instruction manual once filed.
insights.speaker: Palmer Luckey
insights.so_what: A prominent defense-tech CEO is publicly pushing to weaken the disclosure bargain at the core of the US patent system — a live policy risk for any IP-dependent business model.
insights.quote: the entire patent office could be downloaded every single morning and then ripped off and then used to fight a war against you

insights.claim: The US Patent Office reportedly receives about 600,000 applications annually and granted roughly 323,000 (a little over half) in 2025, with total grants up 40% over the last five years — a rise the host attributes in part to AI-assisted patent drafting.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Rising grant volume, plausibly AI-driven, is expanding the patent surface area that Luckey argues is exposed to adversary harvesting.
insights.quote: patents uh uh granted have increased 40% in the last 5 years.

insights.claim: Palmer Luckey proposes massively expanding the Invention Secrecy Act of 1951, under which roughly 6,000 secrecy orders are currently active in the US, so more inventors can obtain classified patents that preserve exclusivity without public disclosure.
insights.speaker: Palmer Luckey
insights.so_what: Watch for legislative or executive action expanding classified-patent programs, which would materially change the calculus for defense-adjacent IP filers.
insights.quote: there roughly 6,000 of these uh secure secrecy orders active in the US.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross rebuts Luckey by clarifying that under the actual Invention Secrecy Act, a confiscated invention can only be practiced by the military for military purposes with royalties to the inventor (not secret commercial exclusivity), and argues expanding it would create dangerous 'secret monopolies' that could bury economically transformative civilian technology, such as in energy, under military classification.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Raises the risk that expanding classified-patent programs could inadvertently suppress civilian economic innovation, not just protect national security.
insights.quote: these are these would be basically secret monopolies

insights.claim: Salim Ismail argues that in an AI-accelerated world, the real competitive moat is no longer patent disclosure or ownership but proprietary 'learning loops' and trade secrets, making continuous innovation rather than IP ownership the winning defensive strategy.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: Suggests investors should weight companies' proprietary data/feedback-loop advantages over patent portfolios when assessing durable competitive moats in AI-era businesses.
insights.quote: Continuous innovation is going to be the winning defense. It's not going to be ownership.

insights.claim: Dave Blundin predicts the US will eventually trade-embargo countries that don't respect intellectual property (contrasting with China, which he says 'completely ignores' IP rights), driven by an accelerating rate of AI-generated inventions that need an enforceable mechanism for innovators to profit.
insights.speaker: Dave Blundin
insights.so_what: A US-led IP trade embargo against non-IP-respecting nations is a scenario to monitor as AI-generated invention volume accelerates.
insights.quote: if there's a flash point for a World War III, this is probably one of the most likely

insights.claim: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 set a new all-time high on HealthBench Professional (OpenAI's hardest medical benchmark, 525 real clinical tasks), and in a blind test of roughly 20,000 individual physician judgments, its answers beat specialty-matched physicians who were given unlimited web access and unlimited time to answer.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Reinforces that AI diagnostic quality now exceeds human specialists even under best-case human conditions, accelerating cost collapse in diagnostics.
insights.quote: who were given unlimited uh full access to the web and unlimited time to answer and the doctors still lost.

insights.claim: Meta's Muse Spark 1.1, released the same week, beat GPT-5.6 on HealthBench Professional while being 7 times cheaper to run, and is distributed free inside WhatsApp, Facebook, and other Meta products reaching 3.56 billion daily active users.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Near-zero-cost, top-tier medical AI advice reaching billions is a structural deflationary shock to healthcare-diagnostics economics, especially in underserved regions.
insights.quote: Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 again released last week uh beat chat GPTs uh or GPT 5.6 Saul on across the marks and it was 7 times cheaper.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross flags likely 'mild benchmaxing' by Meta on the Muse Spark 1.1 HealthBench result, noting per broader AI cost-frontier analysis that Muse Spark sits on the optimal cost frontier but not at its top, and that it still trails models like Fable 5 or GPT-5.6 on tasks such as biological work despite the headline benchmark win.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Headline medical-benchmark wins should be checked against broader capability and cost-frontier context before treating them as a definitive leadership claim.
insights.quote: I suspect a little bit of mild benchmaxing by meta on on this meta spark 1.1

insights.claim: Salim Ismail notes an AI doctor system in China is already being used for diagnosis by over 100 million people in rural environments, illustrating that diagnostic cost collapse has already reached population scale outside the West.
insights.speaker: Salim Ismail
insights.so_what: China's rural AI-doctor deployment at 100M+ users is a real-world precedent for how fast AI diagnostics can scale once cost approaches zero.
insights.quote: there's there's a model in an AI doctor in China that's being used in rural environments by 100 million people already.

insights.claim: A team from startup Revel Pharmaceuticals, working with Alphabet's Calico, published in Nature Communications an engineered enzyme called CMLA that reverses advanced glycation end products (AGEs) — the sugar-protein cross-links long considered a permanent driver of arterial stiffening, cataracts, kidney damage, and wrinkled skin — demonstrating the reversal directly in human tissue samples from elderly donors, not just in vitro.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A category of aging damage previously treated as irreversible now has a demonstrated reversal mechanism in human tissue, a concrete data point supporting a longevity-escape-velocity thesis.
insights.quote: an engineered enzyme called CMLA uh that acts like a molecular lawn mower.

insights.claim: Alex Wisner-Gross contextualizes the Revel/Calico glycation-reversal result within Maillard reaction chemistry (the same reaction that browns bread crust), noting the breakthrough came via directed evolution of a bacteria-derived protein, and asks what other bacterial biochemistry remains unmined for anti-aging applications beyond GLP-1 drugs.
insights.speaker: Alex Wisner-Gross
insights.so_what: Suggests directed evolution of bacterial proteins is an underexplored longevity-research vector worth watching for further breakthroughs.
insights.quote: what else is there out there in the biosphere for us to mine in addition to all the obvious glip ones?
